title: Evaluating Language Models in Realistic Conversational Contexts
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed to serve open-ended, multi-turn interactions, evaluating conversational quality at human scale has become a central challenge. Existing evaluation frameworks built for summarization, translation, or short-form QA tasks fall short of adequately measuring the consistency of human-scale dialogue, especially when derivation and validation of these metrics themselves often rely on synthetic rather than human sources. We fill the gap by introducing \textbf{UPHELD} (Utility \& Planning Human-Scale Evaluated Long Dialogues), a large, reference-full benchmark for evaluating human-scale conversational ability beyond factual correctness. UPHELD consists of hundreds of complete human-to-human dialogues authored by \textbf{professional script writers}, with realistic turn densities and \textbf{36,000+ per-turn human annotations} across \textbf{30,000+ expert-generated dialogue turns}. Using UPHELD, we systematically evaluate classical automatic metrics and reference-free LLM-as-a-judge approaches, and find them unreliable when correlated with expert human judgment. Building off this analysis, we use UPHELD to develop a \textbf{Mixture-of-Judges} framework that combines multiple evaluative signals and improves correlation with human assessments by approximately \textbf{30\%}. Overall, UPHELD provides a robust, human-grounded foundation for evaluating human-scale conversational intelligence that fills a crucial gap in the pre-existing LLM dataset landscape.
decision_comment: The reviewers generally agree that the paper makes a meaningful contribution by introducing a high-quality, human-grounded benchmark for evaluating long-form conversational abilities, and the rebuttal has satisfactorily addressed most of the technical concerns (data construction details, evaluation design, and experimental clarity). Importantly, the ethical issues raised by a reviewer, regarding data sourcing, annotator compensation, and transparency of the data collection process. The authors clarified that all contributions were recruited under transparent contracts (via upwork), compensated at appropriate market rates, and provided informed consent, and that no sensitive personal data is included.  To ensure full transparency and responsible research practice, I explicitly request that the authors consolidate and clearly document these ethical considerations and data governance details in the appendix (or main paper where appropriate) in the camera-ready version.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces UPHELD (Utility & Planning Human-Scale Evaluated Long Dialogues), a novel, reference-full benchmark designed to evaluate the conversational intelligence of Large Language Models (LLMs) in multi-turn, task-oriented settings. UPHELD utilizes professional scriptwriters to craft over multiple expert-generated dialogue turns across hundreds of conversations with extensive per-turn human annotations. Furthermore this paper proposes a Mixture-of-Judges machine learning ensemble utilizing SVM, Linear Regression, and Random Forest over individual metric scores, that improves correlation with human assessments by approximately 30% through combining multiple evaluative signals.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The introduction of UPHELD fills a critical gap in the current evaluation landscape of language model dialogue generation. By employing professional writers, the benchmark ensures high-quality, naturalistic dialogue. 
2. The proposed Mixture-of-Judges ensemble is effective which demonstrates excellent transferability to out-of-domain datasets (Topical-Chat and LLM Arena) when using SVM or Linear Regression.
Weaknesses:
1. There is a contradiction in the text regarding the "Style Equivalence" scale. Section 3.1.2 describes it as a 3-point Likert scale (1-3), while the actual instructions provided to the annotators in Appendix B.2.2 explicitly state it is a 5-point scale.  It would be better to correct this discrepancy.
2. While the dataset is a strong contribution, the proposed "Mixture-of-Judges" evaluation framework simply trains off-the-shelf models (Linear regression, SVM, Random Forest) over standard semantic and token-based metric features, which lacks a novelty contribution.
3. This paper provides relatively little analysis of the proposed Mixture-of-Judges. It would be better to introduce the training process of Mixture of Judges and the analysis of the experimental results, such as experiment setting and robustness analysis of experiment results.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: What specific training objective and hyperparameter tuning were used during tuning custom model in section 5.1, and does its success simply reflect training on the UPHELD distribution?
reviews.limitations: The authors touch upon a few limitations, but they are fragmented and largely buried in the appendices rather than being addressed head-on in the main text. Such as, in Appendix C, the authors acknowledge that their focus is "primarily on English language conversations", and in Appendix J, the authors discuss the limitations of their "Reasonableness" label.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces UPHELD (Utility & Planning Human-Scale Evaluated Long Dialogues), a benchmark designed to evaluate long-form dialogue generation. The dataset consists of 10,000+ expert-generated dialogue turns and includes 36,000+ human annotations assessing content consistency, style consistency, and reasonableness. Using this benchmark, the authors evaluate a range of automatic metrics and show that individual metrics correlate poorly with human judgments in long dialogues. To address this issue, the paper proposes a Mixture-of-Judges ensemble that combines multiple evaluation signals and significantly improves correlation with human ratings. Experiments also demonstrate that the learned evaluator transfers to other dialogue benchmarks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths
- The paper introduces a benchmark consisting of high-quality human-to-human dialogues authored by professional script writers.

- The paper provides a clear motivation for evaluating long, human-scale conversational intelligence.

- The paper provides a comprehensive empirical evaluation and proposes a practical ensemble evaluator that improves correlation with human judgments.

### Weaknesses
- Based on Section 3.1.1, the most recent model included in the evaluation appears to be from 2024. Given the rapid progress in LLMs, it would be helpful if the authors could show whether the failure cases targeted by UPHELD still occur frequently in current SOTA models.

- The paper centers on evaluating long, human-scale conversational intelligence, but many style and coherence issues in practice can be mitigated by carefully designed generation prompts. The manuscript does not systematically report the exact prompts used for the models in Section 3.1.1 during response generation.

- Section 2 provides a detailed overview of recent related work, but no work from 2025 is cited. This raises a concern about whether issues related to fluency and naturalness in LLM-generated text have already been largely addressed by recent advances, or whether they have become less central to current research.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- The most recent model included in the evaluation (Section 3.1.1) appears to be from 2024. Do the failure cases targeted by UPHELD still occur frequently when using more recent SOTA models?

- What exact prompts or system instructions were used when generating responses with the models in Section 3.1.1? In addition, have the authors explored whether carefully designed prompts could significantly improve dialogue coherence and style consistency?

reviews.summary:
This paper introduces a large human-generated multi-turn dialog collection (called UPHELD). It was created by hiring professional script writers on Upwork. Dialogs include factual question answering, task/goal-oriented dialogs, chitchat, context retrieval, summarization and specialized tasks such as code generation and math (Note: Intro suggests they were not included but the appendix says they were A3.1.1 which is confusing).

Using this collection, the authors develop a dialog-turn scoring-metric which is based on an ensemble of token based, semantic and LLM judge based metrics. They find that their method is able to score the human generated response on UPHELD, LLM-Arena and TopicalQA chats better than any of the individual metrics. To calibrate the judges, human annotators are asked to select the better response from pairs consisting of a human-generated response and either a model-generated response or a reference-grounded model paraphrase.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**

- A large collection of human generated dialogs
- Extensive human annotations

**Weakness**
- The quality of the dialogs or how quality control was ensured isn't discussed in the paper (for instance: detecting LLM use by annotators/data generators, ensuring quality and consistency of dialogs etc). 
- Many additional judges have been created but were not - for instance, groundedness judges tend of a lot of variability depending on the LLM used. Could UPHELD have been used to create this?
- Details and statistics on what was actually collected is conflicting/missing  - how many instances per task type? How were seed tasks generated? etc. The introduction suggests data/samples are available in supplementary material but that is not the case.
reviews.limitations: No; there is an impact statement but no discussion about limitations or weaknesses.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a benchmark for the evaluation of dialogue evaluation metrics. The benchmark consists of a collection of dialogues generated by humans. Human annotators were then recruited to annotate automated dialogue completions made by several LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, Llama-3.1-70b, and a fine-tuned Llama-3.1), plus a version that paraphrases the human-generated output, and a version that gives random output, for calibration. This benchmark is then used to evaluate the quality of 12 metrics including token-based (ROUGE), embeddings (BERTScore), and LLM-as-a-judge. The authors highlight the inadequacy of the evaluated metrics, and then propose an ensemble approach that shows higher consistency.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Given the extensive work on LLM-based multi-turn dialogues, this benchmark is very useful to determine and evaluate dialogue evaluation metrics. The paper gives a comprehensive list of existing evaluation frameworks, but unfortunately only a few evaluation metrics

A critical weakness is the choice of evaluation based on ground truth reference used in this approach. The authors attempt to control the possible issue of multiple valid continuations by focusing on task-oriented dialogues. However, even task-oriented dialogues may have multiple valid continuations that may not be covered by the benchmark data, beyond paraphrases, if the task is a complex task. The examples at the end of the appendices clearly illustrate this point. The judges are asked to determine how similar two outputs are, but they are not asked to judge, for example, which output is more natural given the context. As a consequence of this, the evaluation results presented in the paper are not reliable.

The paper shows examples of dialogues generated for the benchmark and a few statistics about the amount of dialogues, turns, and annotations. However, it did not include a detailed characterisation of the types of dialogues, or types of topics used in the dialogues (or I missed them).
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Please provide a more comprehensive justification of the use of the reference-full approach, and discuss limitations of this approach and possible mitigation of these limitations.

The inclusion of the data samples at the end of the appendices is very useful, but please include a detailed description of the dataset and the types of topics used in the generation of the dialogues.
reviews.limitations: No. The paper did not include a section on limitations. Limitations were not discussed explicitly in the paper. The impact statement is very generic, and only focuses on the potential positive aspects of this research.
